estimand_functional: \(\Psi_{\lambda,n}(O_1,\dots,O_n; \hat{\eta}_\lambda) = \hat{\tau}_\lambda\) (clipped AIPW), with target rate \(\rho_n\) and matching minimax converse

assumptions.condition: \(c_- \cdot t^{\kappa} \le P(e(X) \le t) \le c_+ \cdot t^{\kappa}\) for all \(0 < t \le t_0\)
\(\mu_1(x) = g_1(e(x))\) on \(\{x : e(x) \le t_0\}\), with \(g_1 \in H^{\beta}(L_\beta)\)
\(|b_\lambda(x) - \mu_1(x)| \le C_\beta \cdot \lambda^{\beta}\) for almost every \(x\) with \(e(x) \le \lambda\), all \(\lambda \in (0, t_0]\)
Cross-fit nuisance estimates trained on independent folds satisfy \(\mathbb{E}[\, \text{product-drift integrand} \mid \mathcal{T} \,] \le \Delta_n(\lambda)\)
the score, bias, and drift integrands are measurable; the two score integrands are square-integrable

definitions.name: \(P_n\)
inputs: \(H_n\): a probability law for \(U\) on \([0, 1-\eta]\) with \(c_- \cdot t^{\kappa} \le H_n([0,t]) \le c_+ \cdot t^{\kappa}\) for \(0 < t \le t_0\)

statements.route: multiply the pointwise boundary-bias bound (ass:smoothness) by the tail mass P(U ≤ λ) ≲ λᵏ (ass:tail)
statements.proof_tex: On \(\{U \le \lambda\}\) the integrand is bounded by \(C_\beta \lambda^{\beta}\) (ass:smoothness); the tail mass is \(\le c_+ \lambda^{\kappa}\) (ass:tail). Hence the squared \(L_2\) norm is \(\lesssim \lambda^{2\beta+\kappa}\), giving the stated rate.

statements.route: integrate the clipped-tail bias using lem:smoothness-l2 and the tail mass
statements.proof_tex: \(B_\lambda\) integrates the boundary bias against the clipped weight on \(\{U \le \lambda\}\); bounding by lem:smoothness-l2 and the \(\lambda^{\kappa}\) tail mass yields \(\lambda^{\kappa+\beta}\).

statements.statement: \(\hat{\tau}_\lambda - \mathrm{ATE} = G_{\mathrm{or}}(\lambda,n) + G_{\mathrm{plug}}(\lambda,n) + B_\lambda + R_\lambda\), with \(R_\lambda\) second-order
statements.route: expand the clipped AIPW score, apply Neyman orthogonality to drop first-order nuisance error into the second-order remainder
statements.proof_tex: Writing the clipped AIPW score around the oracle and using consistency + exchangeability for the identification of each term, orthogonality (ass:nuisance) sends the first-order nuisance error into \(R_\lambda\); the leading terms are the oracle and plug-in empirical processes plus the deterministic bias \(B_\lambda\).

statements.statement: minimax \(L_1\) risk \(\gtrsim \Delta \cdot (1 - \mathrm{TV}(P_0^n, P_1^n))\) for any two laws separated by \(\Delta\) in the estimand
statements.proof_tex: Standard Le Cam two-point bound (Le Cam 1973; Tsybakov 2009, Thm 2.2): the minimax risk is at least the estimand separation times one minus the total variation between the \(n\)-fold product laws.

statements.route: second-order Taylor / boundedness of the KL Hessian on the quarter band
statements.proof_tex: On \([1/4,3/4]\) the second derivative of \(q \mapsto \mathrm{KL}(\mathrm{Bern}(p) \,\|\, \mathrm{Bern}(q))\) is bounded by 8, so a second-order expansion around \(q=p\) gives the \(4(p-q)^2\) bound.

statements.route: verify each member-property of def:law-class for the construction def:witness
statements.proof_tex: Under \(H_n\), \(P(U \le t) = H_n([0,t])\) satisfies the polynomial tail (so ass:tail holds); the Bernoulli outcome means \(\mu_0=1/2\) and \(\mu_1=g_1\in[1/4,3/4]\) lie in \([0,1]\) (ass:bounded-outcome); and \(\mu_1=g_1\circ e\) with \(g_1\in H^{\beta}(L_\beta)\) gives ass:lower-tail-factorization. Hence \(P_n\) satisfies every defining property of \(\mathcal{P}_{\kappa,\beta}\).

statements.statement: the clipped AIPW estimator at the optimal threshold attains \(\mathbb{E}|\hat{\tau}_\lambda - \mathrm{ATE}| \lesssim \rho_n\)
statements.route: decompose (lem:decomp); bound bias via lem:clipped-bias; control the oracle and plug-in empirical processes at scale I_lambda via the Lindeberg conditions; optimize λ to balance bias and standard deviation, giving rho_n
statements.proof_tex: By lem:decomp the error splits into two empirical processes, the bias \(B_\lambda\), and a second-order remainder. lem:clipped-bias bounds \(|B_\lambda| \lesssim \lambda^{\kappa+\beta}\); the Lindeberg conditions (ass:lindeberg-oracle, ass:lindeberg-plugin) give the empirical processes order \(\sqrt{I_\lambda/n}\); the drift (ass:nuisance) controls the remainder. Optimizing \(\lambda\) balances \(\lambda^{\kappa+\beta}\) against \(\sqrt{I_\lambda/n}\), yielding the rate \(\rho_n\).

statements.route: build a two-point family from def:witness with link functions g1 differing by ≍ λ^β on the tail; both members lie in the class by thm:witness-in-class; separate the ATE by Δ ≍ rho_n and bound the total variation through the Bernoulli KL (lem:bernoulli-kl) and iid tensorization, then apply lem:lecam-two-point
statements.proof_tex: Take \(P_0, P_1\) from def:witness with link functions \(g_1^0, g_1^1 \in [1/4,3/4]\) differing by \(\asymp \lambda^{\beta}\) on \(\{U \le \lambda\}\). Both lie in \(\mathcal{P}_{\kappa,\beta}\) (thm:witness-in-class). Their ATE separation is \(\Delta \asymp \lambda^{\kappa+\beta} \asymp \rho_n\) at the least-favorable \(\lambda\). The per-observation KL is \(\le 4(g_1^0-g_1^1)^2\) on the quarter band (lem:bernoulli-kl), weighted by the \(\lambda^{\kappa}\) tail mass; tensorizing over \(n\) and converting to total variation keeps \(\mathrm{TV}(P_0^n,P_1^n)\) bounded away from 1 at the least-favorable \(\lambda\). lem:lecam-two-point then gives the matching lower bound \(\rho_n\).

sampling_model.draw: iid (O_1, …, O_n) with O_i = (X_i, A_i, Y_i)
sampling_model.cross_fitting: K folds; nuisances trained off-fold; ê_λ(X) ≥ λ and 1 − ê(X) ≥ η̄ on the evaluation fold

bibliography.citation: Imbens & Rubin (2015), Causal Inference for Statistics, Social, and Biomedical Sciences.
Rosenbaum & Rubin (1983), The central role of the propensity score, Biometrika.
Kennedy (2024), Semiparametric doubly robust targeted minimum loss estimation.
D'Amour, Ding, Feller, Lei & Sekhon (2021), Overlap in observational studies with high-dimensional covariates, J. Econometrics.
Audibert & Tsybakov (2007), Fast learning rates for plug-in classifiers, Ann. Statist.
Tsybakov (2009), Introduction to Nonparametric Estimation, Springer.
Chernozhukov et al. (2018), Double/debiased machine learning, Econometrics Journal.
van der Vaart (1998), Asymptotic Statistics, Cambridge University Press.
